news_summary: Breakthroughs in fungal ecology, carbon capture technology, AI-powered wildlife research, and green banking are driving significant environmental progress and economic benefits.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées dans les domaines de l'écologie fongique, des technologies de capture du carbone, de la recherche sur la faune sauvage assistée par l'intelligence artificielle et de la finance verte sont à l'origine de progrès environnementaux significatifs et d'avantages économiques.
news_summary_es: Los avances en ecología fúngica, tecnología de captura de carbono, investigación sobre la fauna silvestre basada en la inteligencia artificial y banca sostenible están impulsando importantes progresos medioambientales y beneficios económicos.

articles.title: Beneath our feet lies a fungal superhighway stretching 68 quadrillion miles
articles.summary: Beneath our feet lies a vast hidden fungal superhighway that helps sustain much of life on Earth—and scientists have now mapped it for the first time. Researchers estimate that these underground networks stretch an astonishing 110 quadrillion kilometers, move about 4 billion tons of carbon dioxide into soils each year, and play a major role in supporting plants and regulating the climate.
articles.content:
Beneath the ground, vast networks of fungi quietly support plant life and play an important role in regulating the planet's climate by helping move carbon into soils. Now, researchers have created the first global maps showing where these underground fungal networks are found and how much of them exist worldwide.

The study, published in Science, focuses on arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi, a group of fungi that form partnerships with most plants on Earth. Alongside the research, scientists released an interactive visualization that allows users to explore the remarkable scale of this hidden underground infrastructure. The maps are expected to help researchers and policymakers identify areas where these fungal networks are thriving and where they may be under threat.

Among the study's key findings:

Global topsoils contain an estimated ~110 quadrillion kilometers (~68 quadrillion miles) of arbuscular mycorrhizal fungal network, made up of thread-like structures called hyphae. That distance is almost a billion times the distance from the Earth to the Sun.

Grasslands contain roughly ~40% of Earth's arbuscular mycorrhizal fungal infrastructure. Particularly dense networks are predicted in the flooded grasslands of South Sudan, the Everglades in Florida, and the Tibetan plateau.

AM fungal networks move an estimated ~4 billion tons of CO2e into soils every year (equivalent to 11% of all human-related carbon-dioxide emissions).

Large agricultural croplands are predicted to have about ~50% lower network densities on average. Researchers caution that less dense fungal networks could reduce a soil's ability to store carbon, cycle nutrients, and withstand environmental stress.

The Hidden Partnerships Supporting Plant Life

Arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi, commonly called AM fungi, form mutually beneficial relationships with approximately ~70% of plant species worldwide. Plants provide the fungi with carbon produced through photosynthesis, while the fungi supply plants with nutrients and water.

These underground networks function as living infrastructure that helps sustain ecosystems and move carbon into the ground. In 2025, researchers published a global analysis of underground mycorrhizal fungal diversity in Nature and launched a digital platform called the Underground Atlas to help identify likely biodiversity hotspots beneath the surface. Until now, however, scientists had not attempted to estimate and map the physical density and worldwide distribution of AM fungal networks themselves.

Mapping 110 Quadrillion Kilometers of Fungal Networks

To build the new maps, researchers compiled measurements from more than 16,000 soil cores collected around the world. They then used machine-learning models that incorporated environmental data from deserts, tundra, forests, and other ecosystems to predict fungal network density in regions where direct measurements were unavailable.

Working with the Physics of Behavior group at the AMOLF research institute, the team also used robotic imaging to analyze more than 300,000 living AM fungal hyphae grown in laboratory conditions. Combining all of these data sources allowed researchers to estimate both the total length and mass of the global network.

Their analysis suggests that AM fungal networks extend for approximately ~110 quadrillion kilometers and contain roughly ~300 megatons of carbon (4-6x the mass of all living humans).

"It is hard to overstate the importance and enormity of these fungi," said lead author Dr. Justin Stewart, with the Society for the Protection of Underground Networks (SPUN). "There could be up to 10 meters (32 feet) of mycorrhizal network in just a teaspoon of soil."

Earth's Underground Circulatory System

Scientists often describe mycorrhizal networks as one of Earth's circulatory systems because they transport carbon, nutrients, and water throughout underground ecosystems.

In healthy soils, these fungal networks can expand the effective foraging area of plant roots by up to 100 times and provide > 80 percent of a plant's phosphorous needs.

"With the emergence of new technologies in high-resolution imaging, machine-learning and robotics, we are starting to reveal what has long been hidden under our feet" said co-lead author, Dr. Corentin Bisot, an AMOLF biophysicist. "We are learning how the complex bodies of network-forming fungi transport nutrients and help regulate the climate."

A New Global Fungal Infrastructure Map

To help visualize the results, the researchers collaborated with award-winning data visualization designer Moritz Stefaner to create the Mycorrhizal Infrastructure Map.

The project offers the most detailed global view yet of Earth's fungal infrastructure. Estimates were calculated for every 1km2 of terrestrial land, excluding ice caps and regions where data were insufficient for reliable predictions.

The data behind the maps are publicly available, giving governments and other decision-makers new tools for monitoring the health of underground fungal communities.

The work builds on previous research published by several of the same authors in Nature. That study examined how mycorrhizal fungi and plants create highly efficient systems for exchanging carbon and nutrients. Researchers measured carbon movement through these living networks at speeds reaching 120 um/sec (if one was inside the network, these speeds would feel like ~400km/hr). The new study extends that work by exploring how these flows operate at a planetary scale.

Threats to Underground Fungal Ecosystems

The researchers also identified areas of concern.

Network densities in croplands are predicted to be about half those found in wild ecosystems. At the same time, wild grasslands contain roughly ~40% of the world's arbuscular mycorrhizal biomass.

Despite their importance, grasslands remain among the least protected ecosystems on Earth and are being converted to agricultural land four times faster than forests.

These findings support previous SPUN research showing that 95% of biodiversity hotspots for arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi lie outside protected areas.

For evolutionary biologist Dr. Toby Kiers, Executive Director of SPUN, the growing body of evidence highlights the need to include fungi in climate and conservation planning.

"Fungi have been ignored in climate and conservation for too long. Now is the time to change that trajectory."

Kiers was recently named a MacArthur Fellow and received the Tyler Prize, often called the "Nobel Prize for the Environment," for her work on plant-fungal relationships.

What Scientists Still Don't Know

"Mycorrhizal fungi have shaped life on earth for hundreds of millions of years, but we still understand too little about how the infrastructure of these living transport systems is distributed across the planet," added co-author and biologist Dr. Merlin Sheldrake. "This study is an exciting step towards understanding how this planetary circulatory system operates and suggests ways that we can better work with fungi to help address many of the unfolding challenges of our times, from food security to climate change."

While the new maps reveal the extraordinary scale of Earth's underground fungal networks, they also highlight major gaps in scientific knowledge. Large regions of the world remain unsampled, providing a roadmap for future research into one of the planet's most important and least visible ecosystems.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in mapping Earth's vast underground fungal networks, revealing their crucial role in carbon cycling, plant support, and climate regulation. This discovery has broad environmental significance, offering new tools for conservation and climate action, and highlights the importance of protecting these ecosystems. The detailed context and data provide a substantive and inspiring advancement in understanding and potentially mitigating climate change.
articles.personality_title: Scientists map Earth's vast underground fungal network for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Underneath the ground, there are huge networks of fungi called arbuscular mycorrhizal (AM) fungi. These fungi connect with most plants and help move carbon into the soil, supporting plant growth and affecting the climate.

**What happened** – Scientists have now created the first global maps showing where these underground fungal networks are and how big they are. Using data from over 16,000 soil samples and advanced computer models, they estimated that these fungi stretch about 110 quadrillion kilometers (68 quadrillion miles) worldwide.

**Impact** – This network is incredibly large—almost a billion times the distance from Earth to the Sun—and moves about 4 billion tons of carbon dioxide into soils each year, which is about 11% of all human-caused emissions. Grasslands hold around 40% of this fungal network, but large farming areas have about half the usual fungal density. This means farming might reduce the soil's ability to store carbon and support plants.

**What's next step** – The research team released interactive maps and data to help scientists and policymakers protect these fungal networks. They also want to study areas that are not yet well understood and work on including fungi in climate and conservation plans.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, scientists have mapped a hidden global fungal network that plays a huge role in supporting plants and storing carbon underground.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques cartographient pour la première fois le vaste réseau fongique souterrain de la Terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Sous nos pieds se trouvent d'immenses réseaux de champignons appelés champignons mycorhiziens arbusculaires (AM). Ces champignons se connectent à la plupart des plantes et aident à déplacer le carbone dans le sol, soutenant la croissance des plantes et influençant le climat.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Les scientifiques ont créé les premières cartes mondiales montrant où se trouvent ces réseaux fongiques souterrains et leur taille. En utilisant des données de plus de 16 000 échantillons de sol et des modèles informatiques avancés, ils ont estimé que ces champignons s'étendent sur environ 110 quadrillions de kilomètres dans le monde.

**Impact** – Ce réseau est incroyablement grand – presque un milliard de fois la distance entre la Terre et le Soleil – et déplace environ 4 milliards de tonnes de dioxyde de carbone dans les sols chaque année, soit environ 11 % des émissions humaines. Les prairies contiennent environ 40 % de ce réseau fongique, mais les grandes zones agricoles ont environ la moitié de la densité fongique normale. Cela signifie que l'agriculture pourrait réduire la capacité du sol à stocker le carbone et à soutenir les plantes.

**Prochaine étape** – L'équipe de recherche a publié des cartes interactives et des données pour aider les scientifiques et les décideurs à protéger ces réseaux fongiques. Ils souhaitent aussi étudier les zones encore peu explorées et intégrer les champignons dans les plans climatiques et de conservation.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, les scientifiques ont cartographié un réseau fongique mondial caché qui joue un rôle majeur dans le soutien des plantes et le stockage du carbone sous terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos mapean por primera vez la vasta red fúngica subterránea de la Tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Bajo nuestros pies existen enormes redes de hongos llamados hongos micorrízicos arbusculares (AM). Estos hongos se conectan con la mayoría de las plantas y ayudan a mover carbono al suelo, apoyando el crecimiento de las plantas y afectando el clima.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos crearon los primeros mapas globales que muestran dónde están estas redes fúngicas subterráneas y qué tan grandes son. Usaron datos de más de 16,000 muestras de suelo y modelos informáticos avanzados para estimar que estos hongos se extienden aproximadamente 110 cuatrillones de kilómetros en todo el mundo.

**Impacto** – Esta red es increíblemente grande — casi mil millones de veces la distancia de la Tierra al Sol — y mueve alrededor de 4 mil millones de toneladas de dióxido de carbono al suelo cada año, lo que equivale al 11 % de todas las emisiones causadas por humanos. Las praderas contienen cerca del 40 % de esta red fúngica, pero las grandes áreas agrícolas tienen aproximadamente la mitad de la densidad normal de hongos. Esto significa que la agricultura podría reducir la capacidad del suelo para almacenar carbono y apoyar a las plantas.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de investigación lanzó mapas interactivos y datos para ayudar a científicos y responsables a proteger estas redes fúngicas. También quieren estudiar áreas poco conocidas y trabajar para incluir a los hongos en planes de conservación y climáticos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, los científicos han mapeado una red fúngica global oculta que juega un papel importante en el apoyo a las plantas y en el almacenamiento de carbono bajo tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an expansive underground network of glowing, thread-like fungal hyphae weaving through rich soil beneath a vibrant, thriving grassland landscape, with subtle symbolic flows of light and carbon particles moving along the fungal threads, illustrating the hidden fungal superhighway sustaining plant life and the planet’s climate.

articles.title: Scientists crack a decades-old CO2 problem and triple fuel production
articles.summary: A new catalyst design could significantly improve the conversion of CO2 into methanol, an important fuel and chemical feedstock. Researchers separated key reaction steps across different catalyst sites, avoiding a long-standing trade-off between speed and efficiency. The result was about three times more methanol production than standard commercial catalysts.
articles.content:
Converting carbon dioxide (CO 2 ) into methanol is widely viewed as a promising way to recycle carbon resources. However, scientists have long faced a difficult challenge when trying to improve the process.

At lower temperatures, converting CO2 into methanol is thermodynamically favorable. The problem is that CO2 becomes difficult to activate under these conditions, resulting in weak catalytic performance. Raising the temperature speeds up the reaction, but it also encourages a competing process known as the reverse water-gas shift reaction, which produces unwanted byproducts and lowers methanol selectivity. This persistent trade-off between catalytic activity and selectivity has limited progress in increasing methanol yields.

New Catalyst Design Overcomes Long-Standing Trade-Off

In a study published in Chem, researchers led by Prof. Jian Sun and Prof. Jiafeng Yu of the Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics (DICP) at the Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) developed a new catalyst design aimed at addressing this challenge.

Their approach uses a strong metal-support interaction (SMSI)-driven overlayer structure to spatially separate active sites within the catalyst. This design allows different reaction steps to occur in different locations, improving the efficiency of methanol production from CO 2 .

By restructuring the catalyst surface and changing how reactants adsorb, dissociate, and move through the reaction pathway, the team achieved a space-time yield of 1.2 g·g cat -1·h-1 at 300 ℃ and 3 MPa. That performance is approximately three times higher than that of conventional commercial Cu/Zn/Al catalysts.

Redirecting CO 2 Toward Methanol

The researchers found that their catalyst encourages CO 2 to adsorb and activate primarily on zirconia (ZrO 2 ) sites. This steers the reaction toward methanol production through the formate pathway.

In conventional Cu-based catalysts, activation typically begins by breaking the C=O bond before hydrogenation occurs. The new strategy follows a different sequence. Hydrogenation takes place first on ZrO 2 sites, and C=O bond cleavage occurs afterward.

According to the researchers, this change in reaction mechanism significantly reduces the formation of carbon monoxide (CO) byproducts while preserving the strong ability of Cu sites to dissociate H 2 efficiently.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in catalyst design that triples methanol production from CO2, addressing a major challenge in carbon recycling. This advancement has broad environmental implications by improving sustainable fuel production and reducing harmful byproducts, benefiting society at large.
articles.personality_title: New catalyst triples methanol fuel made from carbon dioxide
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been trying to turn carbon dioxide (CO2) into methanol, a useful fuel and chemical, in a way that is both fast and efficient. Normally, making methanol from CO2 is tricky because at low temperatures the process is slow, and at high temperatures it creates unwanted byproducts.

**What happened** – Researchers at the Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics in China created a new catalyst that separates different reaction steps onto different parts of the catalyst. This design helps the reaction happen more smoothly and avoids the usual problems. They tested it at 300 degrees Celsius and 3 megapascals pressure, and the catalyst produced about three times more methanol than regular catalysts.

**Impact** – This new catalyst changes how CO2 molecules react, making them turn into methanol more directly and reducing harmful byproducts like carbon monoxide. The result is a much better way to recycle CO2 into fuel, which could help reduce pollution and create cleaner energy sources.

**What's next step** – Scientists will likely continue testing this catalyst to improve it further and explore ways to use it in real factories. This could lead to more efficient and environmentally friendly production of methanol and other chemicals from CO2.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new catalyst design greatly improves the process of turning carbon dioxide into methanol fuel by tripling production and reducing waste.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau catalyseur triple la production de méthanol à partir du dioxyde de carbone
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent depuis longtemps à transformer le dioxyde de carbone (CO2) en méthanol, un carburant et produit chimique utile, de manière rapide et efficace. Habituellement, cette transformation est difficile car à basse température le processus est lent, et à haute température il produit des sous-produits indésirables.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Institut de physique chimique de Dalian en Chine ont créé un nouveau catalyseur qui sépare différentes étapes de la réaction sur différentes parties du catalyseur. Cette conception aide la réaction à se dérouler plus facilement et évite les problèmes habituels. Testé à 300 degrés Celsius et 3 mégapascals de pression, ce catalyseur a produit environ trois fois plus de méthanol que les catalyseurs classiques.

**Impact** – Ce nouveau catalyseur modifie la façon dont les molécules de CO2 réagissent, les transformant plus directement en méthanol et réduisant les sous-produits nocifs comme le monoxyde de carbone. Cela offre une meilleure méthode pour recycler le CO2 en carburant, ce qui pourrait aider à réduire la pollution et créer des sources d'énergie plus propres.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement continuer à tester ce catalyseur pour l'améliorer et étudier comment l'utiliser dans des usines réelles. Cela pourrait permettre une production plus efficace et écologique de méthanol et d'autres produits chimiques à partir du CO2.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle conception de catalyseur améliore grandement la transformation du dioxyde de carbone en carburant méthanol en triplant la production et en réduisant les déchets.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo catalizador triplica la producción de metanol a partir de dióxido de carbono
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han intentado durante mucho tiempo convertir el dióxido de carbono (CO2) en metanol, un combustible y producto químico útil, de manera rápida y eficiente. Normalmente, este proceso es complicado porque a bajas temperaturas es lento, y a altas temperaturas se producen subproductos no deseados.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Instituto de Física Química de Dalian en China crearon un nuevo catalizador que separa diferentes pasos de la reacción en distintas partes del catalizador. Este diseño ayuda a que la reacción ocurra de forma más fluida y evita los problemas habituales. Lo probaron a 300 grados Celsius y 3 megapascales de presión, y el catalizador produjo alrededor de tres veces más metanol que los catalizadores comunes.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo catalizador cambia cómo reaccionan las moléculas de CO2, convirtiéndolas en metanol de manera más directa y reduciendo subproductos dañinos como el monóxido de carbono. Esto ofrece una mejor forma de reciclar CO2 en combustible, lo que podría ayudar a reducir la contaminación y crear fuentes de energía más limpias.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos probablemente seguirán probando este catalizador para mejorarlo y explorar formas de usarlo en fábricas reales. Esto podría llevar a una producción más eficiente y ecológica de metanol y otros productos químicos a partir del CO2.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo diseño de catalizador mejora mucho el proceso de convertir dióxido de carbono en combustible de metanol al triplicar la producción y reducir los residuos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an abstract catalyst structure resembling intertwined metallic and zirconia crystal lattices glowing softly with blue and copper hues, with delicate streams of translucent CO2 molecules flowing smoothly into vibrant, radiant methanol droplets forming beneath, set against a simple, natural-toned background evoking scientific innovation and clean energy transformation.

articles.title: How AI is revealing the secret lives of animals from hummingbirds to pumas
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 15 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01887-wAdvances in machine learning and other technologies are helping researchers to trace the movements, landmarks and social practices of wildlife.
articles.content:
Peering out at California’s San Gabriel Mountains through the windows of my cabin, I can still recall the massive wildfire that incinerated more than 40,000 hectares of Angeles National Forest in 2020. As colossal columns of smoke rose above the ridge line, newsreaders reported that no homes had been destroyed and that the blaze had not caused any injuries. That was blatantly untrue.

Countless forest residents — from rabbits and squirrels to coyotes (Canis latrans) and pumas (Puma concolor) — were killed or maimed in the megafire, and the areas they once called home were reduced to ash. However, it seemed that these casualties didn’t count, because only places where humans live were deemed inhabited. By contrast, the millions of animal species we share the planet with — what naturalist Henry Beston called the “other nations” of our world in his book The Outermost House (1928) — are too often dismissed as living brief and transitory lives in the supposedly uninhabited wilderness.

AI is helping to decode animals’ speech. Will it also let us talk with them?

This distinction between humans and the rest of the animal kingdom is proving increasingly difficult to defend. Fresh insights into the deep relationships that animals have with the land, air and sea are emerging from ambitious studies that combine GPS data, precise satellite imagery and artificial-intelligence systems.

As well as tracking animal movements, these technologies are revealing the relationships, resources, conflict zones, knowledge hubs, borders and landmarks that give all creatures a ‘sense of place’. Such knowledge can empower wildlife-conservation efforts. Ultimately, these advances could enable humanity to “listen to the animals and hear what they have to say”, notes Jewlya Samaniego, an ethnobotanist at the Wallis Annenberg Wildlife Crossing Native Plant Nursery in Agoura Hills, California. GPS tracking data of pumas and other animals have helped to demonstrate the need to build Samaniego’s ‘vegetated bridge’, which will be the largest wildlife crossing on Earth when it opens in December.

Here are more ways technology is rewriting humanity’s relationship with the natural world.

Fire hose of data

A generation ago, a zoologist would have been delighted to receive a location signal from a GPS tracker once a day. Now, an ultralight tag on a mammal or bird can broadcast its location every second. Moreover, 3D maps of such data are revealing sophisticated and often surprising behavioural patterns.

For example, after years of fieldwork, behavioural ecologist Meg Crofoot and her colleagues at the Max Planck Institute of Animal Behavior in Konstanz, Germany, concluded that kinkajous (Potos flavus) in the Panamanian rainforest return to the same fruit trees for food every day, suggesting that these trees must be key landmarks on the arboreal animals’ cognitive map. But modern tags have revealed much more: the kinkajous also seem to travel across the forest by walking along the exact same branches — a network of well-established aerial trails runs through the treetops.

This finding raises another question: how do they move so deliberately? Crofoot’s current theory is that the kinkajous might scent-mark their preferred trails like ants do, transforming the forest into a lattice of invisible viaducts.

A wild kinkajou (Potos flavus) reaches for the flower of a balsa tree (Ochroma pyramidale).Credit: Christian Ziegler

Fellow Max Planck ecologist Martin Wikelski is scaling up GPS tracking, aiming to follow potentially millions of animals worldwide, from antelopes to falcons, with the goal of monitoring the “pulse of the living planet”. His international initiative, ICARUS, uses tiny tags and powerful receivers installed on shoebox-sized satellites called CubeSats.

CubeSat launches are now under way, and in May, I joined Wikelski at the Vandenberg Space Force Base in California to watch one depart for its new home, 500 kilometres above the animals it will observe. Others will be sent up over the next 18 months to achieve global coverage. Wikelski anticipates that this network could track members of at least 40% of all bird species and 50% of mammalian species. For researchers studying animal behaviour, this will be like turning on the lights in a dark room.

What is the future of intelligence? The answer could lie in the story of its evolution

Wikelski aims to monitor disturbances in movement patterns in real time — like an interspecies surveillance system. Animals fleeing an area might reveal issues worth investigating, such as natural resource depletion, poaching or a disease. Humans might even be able to better predict little-understood phenomena, such as earthquakes and volcanic eruptions, through changes in wildlife behaviour.

Even at this early stage, ICARUS and similar projects are generating a fire hose of data on animal movements. Most flows into a database called Movebank, maintained by the Max Planck institute. As of April, Movebank has logged nearly 11 billion animal locations, spanning more than 1,600 species. The database grows by nearly 12 million records each day — creating a vast collection for humans and machines to potentially analyse.

Superintelligent eyes and ears

Back on the ground, technologies are listening and watching as animals navigate their worlds. Motion-sensing cameras and finely tuned acoustic devices have long served as valuable tools for spying on animals as they pass through an area. Historically, biologists had to review these recordings frame by frame or use crowdsourced volunteers, limiting how much information could be gleaned from the data.

Now, AI can handle much of that hard work, rapidly identifying and logging animals from hummingbirds to rhinoceroses by sound or appearance, and pinging researchers’ phones whenever their species of interest is spotted.

Such systems can also assess whole animal populations. A trained AI tool took some 50 hours to analyse around 45 hours of raw camera footage obtained at the Kasanka National Park in Zambia, counting the number of straw-coloured fruit bats (Eidolon helvum) flying around. The average over five days: 857,233 bats. That makes the colony one of Africa’s largest. It would have taken humans an estimated 13 years to do the analysis or, more likely, it wouldn’t have been done at all.

But such population counts matter, scientists say, because these fruit bats disperse seeds over distances of more than 75 kilometres — three times farther than an African elephant (Loxodonta spp.) does. The larger the colony, the more seeds are dispersed. In other words, counting bats is a measure of the health of the entire ecosystem.

Straw-coloured fruit bats (Eidolon helvum) disperse seeds over long distances, providing crucial ecosystem services.Credit: Nick Garbutt/Nature Picture Library

AI-powered surveillance tools can also help land managers to identify and reduce conflicts between people and wildlife. When an analysis showed that fences along Scottish rail lines were disrupting the passage of local hedgehogs (Erinaceus spp.), workers cut small holes — dubbed hedgehog highways — in the fencing to allow the animals through.

AI has similarly supercharged the once-tedious work of scanning satellite imagery for signs of ecosystem engineering. In 2007, ecologist Jean Thie at EcoInformatics in Wakefield, Canada, manually scoured high-resolution images to identify Earth’s longest beaver dam — which stretches for more than 800 metres. From the ground, it’s hard to spot the dam and the area simply looks like a large pond, whereas, viewed from above, the beaver-engineered canals and family lodges are clearly visible.

Birds get a bad rap: why we should look up to our feathered friends

Nearly two decades on, AI has begun to automate Thie’s successful technique for identifying beaver homesteads. Geographer Emily Fairfax, now at the University of Minnesota in Minneapolis, worked with a team at Google in Mountain View, California, to develop EEAGER, a machine-learning tool for identifying beaver dams1. Fairfax and her colleagues have shown that the animals’ presence is linked to enhanced biodiversity, improved water quality, wildfire mitigation and carbon sequestration.

Furthermore, camera-equipped drones have been used to conduct instant animal censuses. For example, field researchers can fly aerial cameras over a popular watering hole and then use algorithms to identify the species gathered there and estimate the age and sex of the individuals. No tracking tags, no traps — just eyes in the sky trained in what to look for.

In some cases, instead of merely labelling a striped mammal as a zebra (Equus spp.), an AI system can even identify exactly which individual it has spotted2. These tools can also work out whether animal groups are visiting an area to forage or whether their movements are part of a well-established transit corridor. They do so by studying the animals’ behaviour together with environmental features, such as landscape topography and wildlife trails3. These clues collectively reveal not just who lives where, but why.

Crowdsourced connections
articles.reasoning: The article describes how AI and advanced technologies are enabling large-scale, detailed tracking and understanding of wildlife behavior and ecosystems, which has significant positive implications for conservation, biodiversity, and ecosystem health. The impact is broad, affecting many species and habitats globally, and the article provides substantial context and examples of how these technologies are transforming wildlife research and conservation efforts.
articles.personality_title: How AI is helping scientists understand animal lives and protect nature
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied animals to learn about their habits and environments. But tracking many animals and understanding their behaviors over large areas has been very hard. New technologies like GPS trackers, satellites, and artificial intelligence (AI) are now changing how we study wildlife.

**What happened** – Researchers are using AI and tiny GPS devices to follow animals such as pumas, hummingbirds, and kinkajous in real time. Satellites called CubeSats orbit Earth to collect data from these trackers, covering many species worldwide. AI also helps analyze photos and sounds from cameras and microphones to identify animals quickly and count populations, like hundreds of thousands of fruit bats in Africa.

**Impact** – This technology reveals how animals move, find food, mark trails, and interact with their environment. For example, kinkajous use the same tree branches repeatedly, suggesting they have special paths in the forest. Knowing these details helps protect animals by guiding efforts like building wildlife crossings and improving habitats. It also shows how animals respond to dangers like wildfires or human fences, allowing better conservation planning.

**What's next step** – More satellites will launch soon to track more animals globally. Scientists plan to use this data to detect changes in animal behavior that might warn of problems like poaching or environmental damage. AI tools will continue to improve, making it easier to monitor wildlife and support efforts to keep ecosystems healthy.

**One-sentence takeaway** – AI and advanced tracking technologies are giving scientists new ways to understand and protect animals by revealing their hidden lives and movements across the planet.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment l’IA aide les scientifiques à comprendre la vie des animaux et protéger la nature
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient les animaux pour comprendre leurs habitudes et leurs environnements. Mais suivre de nombreux animaux sur de vastes territoires était très difficile. Les nouvelles technologies comme les traceurs GPS, les satellites et l’intelligence artificielle (IA) changent cette approche.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les chercheurs utilisent l’IA et de petits traceurs GPS pour suivre en temps réel des animaux comme les pumas, les colibris et les kinkajous. Des satellites appelés CubeSats orbitent autour de la Terre pour collecter ces données, couvrant de nombreuses espèces dans le monde. L’IA aide aussi à analyser rapidement les photos et sons provenant de caméras et micros pour identifier et compter les animaux, comme des centaines de milliers de chauves-souris en Afrique.

**Impact** – Ces technologies révèlent comment les animaux se déplacent, trouvent leur nourriture, marquent leurs chemins et interagissent avec leur environnement. Par exemple, les kinkajous utilisent toujours les mêmes branches, ce qui suggère qu’ils ont des sentiers spéciaux dans la forêt. Ces informations aident à protéger les animaux en guidant la construction de passages pour la faune et en améliorant les habitats. Elles montrent aussi comment les animaux réagissent aux dangers, comme les incendies ou les clôtures humaines.

**Prochaine étape** – D’autres satellites seront bientôt lancés pour suivre encore plus d’animaux dans le monde. Les scientifiques veulent utiliser ces données pour détecter des changements dans le comportement des animaux qui pourraient signaler des problèmes comme le braconnage ou la destruction de l’environnement. Les outils d’IA continueront à s’améliorer pour faciliter la surveillance de la faune et soutenir la protection des écosystèmes.

**Phrase clé** – L’IA et les technologies avancées offrent aux scientifiques de nouvelles façons de comprendre et protéger les animaux en révélant leur vie cachée et leurs déplacements à travers la planète.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo la IA ayuda a los científicos a entender la vida de los animales y proteger la naturaleza
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado a los animales para aprender sobre sus hábitos y entornos. Pero seguir a muchos animales en grandes áreas ha sido muy difícil. Nuevas tecnologías como rastreadores GPS, satélites e inteligencia artificial (IA) están cambiando la forma en que estudiamos la vida silvestre.

**Qué pasó** – Los investigadores usan IA y pequeños dispositivos GPS para seguir en tiempo real a animales como pumas, colibríes y kinkajúes. Satélites llamados CubeSats orbitan la Tierra para recoger datos de estos rastreadores, cubriendo muchas especies en todo el mundo. La IA también ayuda a analizar fotos y sonidos de cámaras y micrófonos para identificar animales rápidamente y contar poblaciones, como cientos de miles de murciélagos en África.

**Impacto** – Esta tecnología muestra cómo los animales se mueven, encuentran comida, marcan caminos e interactúan con su entorno. Por ejemplo, los kinkajúes usan las mismas ramas repetidamente, lo que sugiere que tienen rutas especiales en el bosque. Conocer estos detalles ayuda a proteger a los animales, guiando la construcción de pasos para vida silvestre y mejorando los hábitats. También muestra cómo reaccionan a peligros como incendios o cercas humanas.

**Próximo paso** – Se lanzarán más satélites pronto para rastrear más animales en todo el mundo. Los científicos planean usar estos datos para detectar cambios en el comportamiento animal que podrían advertir sobre problemas como la caza furtiva o daños ambientales. Las herramientas de IA seguirán mejorando para facilitar la vigilancia de la vida silvestre y apoyar la salud de los ecosistemas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La IA y las tecnologías avanzadas están dando a los científicos nuevas formas de entender y proteger a los animales al revelar sus vidas y movimientos ocultos por todo el planeta.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a puma and a hummingbird connected by glowing, intricate lines symbolizing AI data streams, set against the softly lit silhouette of the San Gabriel Mountains with subtle satellite and GPS motifs woven into the natural landscape, using gentle earth tones and muted blues.

articles.title: 11,000 jobs, $1.4 billion in savings: what a decade of green banking built in Philadelphia
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM An impressive $14.7 million from the city. $1.3 billion in economic investment returned. $1.4 billion in energy savings. 11,000 jobs created. Those are the results of the Philadelphia Energy Campaign’s first decade, according to a 10-year economic impact report released last month. The financing model that made this possible […]
The post 11,000 jobs, $1.4 billion in savings: what a decade of green banking built in Philadelphia first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

An impressive $14.7 million from the city. $1.3 billion in economic investment returned. $1.4 billion in energy savings. 11,000 jobs created. Those are the results of the Philadelphia Energy Campaign’s first decade, according to a 10-year economic impact report released last month.

The financing model that made this possible

Emily Schapira, president and CEO of the Philadelphia Energy Authority (PEA), describes the core approach as stacking. “The stacking and layering of different types of funding is really the unique thing that’s happening in Philadelphia that I think is not the norm,” she says. “We’re able to blend private financing with a little bit of public or a little bit of philanthropy and turn that into something that everybody can use. That’s the model.”

Two tools have been central to that stack. Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy, known as C-PACE, lets property owners finance energy upgrades through a voluntary property tax bill assessment; roughly $400 million in C-PACE financing supported 22 projects and produced $29.9 million in municipal tax revenue. The Philadelphia Green Capital Corp., a nonprofit green bank PEA launched in 2021, provides low-cost financing for efficiency and renewable projects, giving the model a vehicle for blending dollars that would otherwise stay separate.

Programs that delivered the numbers

More than 130,000 LED street lights were installed. The Philadelphia Museum of Art cut electricity use by 28 percent through efficiency upgrades. Built to Last, a program that aggregates available funding to help low-income homeowners access repair and energy programs, has reached more than 400 households, delivering an estimated $300 to $1,000 in annual savings each.

Solarize Greater Philadelphia helped more than 4,300 homeowners install solar, more than half of them low-income, adding 25 megawatts to the grid. A water and sewer line protection program reduces the energy the water department spends on pumping and treating water lost through leaky pipes.

What political support accomplished

The campaign launched in 2016 with backing from Philadelphia City Council President Darrell Clarke, who assembled a coalition of labor, school officials, and community organizations. Schapira is direct about what that unlocked. “Having that political encouragement, even though it didn’t come with a lot of money, really made all the difference,” she says. “It opened a lot of doors. It got different parts of government, like our housing authority and our transit authority and our school district and folks who don’t always get to work together in productive ways, to really open their minds to it.”

Schapira calls the early strategy a Jenga approach: “just tapping the blocks to see which ones come loose.” Projects were chosen based on what was moveable, not what looked most ambitious.

Built without depending on federal dollars

The campaign launched during the first Trump administration, when federal clean energy funding was not a safe assumption. That starting condition shaped the entire design. The loss of a $156 million Solar for All grant has been painful, but Schapira says resilience was built in from the start. “We really designed everything around models that are very flexible and able to take advantage of opportunities when they come.”

Philadelphia sustainability director Elizabeth Lankenau distills the wider lesson. “We’re all moving the needle in our own way,” she says. “So, let’s just keep doing what we’re doing and focus on what we can change.” Across ten years, Philadelphia changed quite a lot.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a decade-long green banking initiative in Philadelphia that created 11,000 jobs, generated $1.4 billion in energy savings, and attracted $1.3 billion in economic investment. It highlights a successful financing model blending public, private, and philanthropic funds to support energy efficiency and renewable projects with broad societal and environmental benefits. The story provides detailed context on programs, political support, and resilience without federal funding, demonstrating significant positive impact on the community and environment.
articles.personality_title: Philadelphia’s 10-year green campaign creates 11,000 jobs and saves $1.4 billion in energy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Philadelphia launched a city-wide effort in 2016 called the Philadelphia Energy Campaign to improve energy efficiency and support renewable energy. This campaign aimed to reduce energy costs and promote green projects without relying heavily on federal funding.

**What happened** – Over ten years, the campaign used a special financing method called "stacking," which blends private money, public funds, and donations to pay for energy upgrades. Tools like C-PACE, a program that helps property owners finance improvements through their property taxes, and the Philadelphia Green Capital Corp., a nonprofit green bank, were key. The campaign installed over 130,000 LED street lights, helped thousands of homeowners add solar panels, and supported energy-saving projects in public buildings.

**Impact** – The campaign resulted in 11,000 new jobs, $1.4 billion saved on energy bills, and $1.3 billion returned in economic investment. It also helped low-income families save money and reduced electricity use at major places like the Philadelphia Museum of Art by 28%. Importantly, the campaign succeeded despite limited federal support, showing a new model for funding green projects.

**What's next step** – The city plans to keep using flexible financing strategies and partnerships to continue improving energy efficiency and expanding renewable energy. This approach can adapt to new opportunities and may inspire other cities to try similar models.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Philadelphia’s decade-long green energy campaign created thousands of jobs and saved billions by using a smart mix of funding to support energy upgrades and clean projects without relying on federal money.
articles.personality_title_fr: La campagne verte de Philadelphie crée 11 000 emplois et économise 1,4 milliard de dollars en énergie en 10 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Philadelphie a lancé en 2016 une campagne appelée Philadelphia Energy Campaign pour améliorer l’efficacité énergétique et soutenir les énergies renouvelables, sans dépendre fortement des fonds fédéraux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Pendant dix ans, la campagne a utilisé un système de financement appelé « empilement », qui mélange argent privé, public et dons pour payer les améliorations énergétiques. Des outils comme C-PACE, qui aide les propriétaires à financer les travaux via leurs taxes foncières, et la Philadelphia Green Capital Corp., une banque verte à but non lucratif, ont été essentiels. Plus de 130 000 lampadaires LED ont été installés, des milliers de foyers ont ajouté des panneaux solaires, et des projets dans des bâtiments publics ont réduit la consommation d’énergie.

**Impact** – La campagne a créé 11 000 emplois, économisé 1,4 milliard de dollars sur les factures d’énergie et généré 1,3 milliard de dollars d’investissements économiques. Elle a aussi aidé des familles à faibles revenus à économiser de l’argent et réduit la consommation d’électricité du Philadelphia Museum of Art de 28 %. Ce succès, obtenu malgré un soutien fédéral limité, montre un nouveau modèle pour financer les projets verts.

**Prochaine étape** – La ville prévoit de continuer à utiliser ces stratégies de financement flexibles et des partenariats pour améliorer encore l’efficacité énergétique et développer les énergies renouvelables. Ce modèle pourrait inspirer d’autres villes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La campagne énergétique verte de Philadelphie a créé des milliers d’emplois et économisé des milliards en combinant intelligemment plusieurs sources de financement pour soutenir des projets propres, sans dépendre de l’État fédéral.
articles.personality_title_es: La campaña verde de Filadelfia crea 11,000 empleos y ahorra 1.4 mil millones en energía en 10 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Filadelfia lanzó en 2016 una campaña llamada Philadelphia Energy Campaign para mejorar la eficiencia energética y apoyar la energía renovable, sin depender mucho de fondos federales.

**Qué pasó** – Durante diez años, la campaña usó un método de financiamiento llamado "apilamiento", que combina dinero privado, público y donaciones para pagar mejoras energéticas. Herramientas como C-PACE, que ayuda a los propietarios a financiar mejoras a través de sus impuestos a la propiedad, y Philadelphia Green Capital Corp., un banco verde sin fines de lucro, fueron clave. Se instalaron más de 130,000 luces LED, miles de hogares añadieron paneles solares y se apoyaron proyectos para ahorrar energía en edificios públicos.

**Impacto** – La campaña creó 11,000 empleos, ahorró 1.4 mil millones de dólares en facturas de energía y generó 1.3 mil millones en inversión económica. También ayudó a familias de bajos ingresos a ahorrar dinero y redujo el uso de electricidad en el Museo de Arte de Filadelfia en un 28 %. Este éxito, logrado con poco apoyo federal, muestra un nuevo modelo para financiar proyectos verdes.

**Próximo paso** – La ciudad planea seguir usando estas estrategias flexibles de financiamiento y asociaciones para mejorar la eficiencia energética y expandir la energía renovable. Este enfoque podría inspirar a otras ciudades.

**Resumen en una frase** – La campaña energética verde de Filadelfia creó miles de empleos y ahorró miles de millones combinando de forma inteligente varias fuentes de financiamiento para apoyar proyectos limpios, sin depender del gobierno federal.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a vibrant Philadelphia cityscape at dusk, featuring stylized glowing LED streetlights lining the streets, solar panels integrated on building rooftops, and abstract layered blocks stacked like a Jenga tower symbolizing the innovative financing model, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and soft greens to evoke growth and sustainability.
